title: Inside the Cortical Decoder v3 Architecture
excerpt: A deep dive into the transformer-based neural decoding pipeline that powers real-time intent classification at sub-millisecond latency — from raw spike trains to actionable motor commands.
body:
## The Challenge

Decoding human motor intent from raw neural spike trains in real time is one of the hardest problems in computational neuroscience. Our v3 architecture represents a fundamental rethinking of the pipeline — moving from traditional Kalman filters to a hybrid transformer model that processes 512 channels simultaneously.

## Architecture Overview

The Cortical Decoder v3 is built around three core stages:

1. **Spike Sorting & Feature Extraction** — Raw electrode signals are processed through our NeurSort engine, extracting spike waveforms and computing firing rates across all channels in under 2ms.

2. **Temporal Attention Module** — A custom transformer encoder attends to 200ms sliding windows of neural activity, capturing complex temporal dependencies that traditional linear decoders miss entirely.

3. **Intent Classification Head** — The final stage maps encoded representations to a 64-dimensional action space covering cursor movement, click intent, and text selection.

## Performance Benchmarks

| Metric | v2 (Kalman) | v3 (Transformer) |
|--------|-------------|-------------------|
| Decode Latency | 12ms | 0.8ms |
| Classification Accuracy | 89.2% | 97.6% |
| Channel Support | 128 | 512 |
| Power Consumption | 45mW | 18mW |

## Key Innovation: Sparse Attention

The breakthrough that made sub-millisecond latency possible is our sparse attention mechanism. Instead of computing full self-attention across all time steps, we use a learned sparsity pattern that focuses computation on the most informative neural events.

This reduces the computational complexity from O(n²) to O(n log n) while maintaining 99.1% of the full-attention accuracy.

## What's Next

We're currently extending the v3 architecture to support multi-region decoding — simultaneously reading from motor cortex, premotor cortex, and supplementary motor areas. Early results from our 128-patient study show a further 34% improvement in complex motor task decoding.

The Cortical Decoder v3 is available to clinical partners through our Early Access Program. If you're working on brain-computer interface research, [reach out to our team](/contact) to discuss integration.
